subject: Chemistry
q: Total number of relatively more stable isomer(s) possible for octahedral complex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Cu</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>en</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mfenced><mi>SCN</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> will be____
question: Total number of relatively more stable isomer(s) possible for octahedral complex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Cu</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>en</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mfenced><mi>SCN</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> will be____
solution:
This octahedral complex is of M(AA)2b2 type structure in which AA is bidentate ligand and b is monodentate ligand. This can exist in two geometrical isomeric form cis and trans. Out of which trans form is relatively more stable isomer. Now due to ambidentate ligand SCN- it can also shows linkage isomersim.

Cuen2SCN2

So, relatively more stable trans isomers will have three possible isomers.

q: The magnetic moment is measured in Bohr Magneton (BM). Spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> complexes respectively is:
question: The magnetic moment is measured in Bohr Magneton (BM). Spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> complexes respectively is:
solution:
The spin only magnetic moment can be calculated as follows

μ=nn+2BM

n=Number of unpaired electrons

FeH2O63+=d5(High spin complex)=5.unpaired electrons
μ=5×7=35
=5.92 B.M.
FeCN63+=d5(Low spin complex)=1
μ=1×3=3
=1.732 B.M.

Among the given complexes, number of paramagnetic complexes is
question:
FeCN64-

FeCN63-

TiCN63-

NiCN42-

CoCN63-

Among the given complexes, number of paramagnetic complexes is
solution:
Complexes in which central atom has unpaired electrons is said to be paramagnetic in nature and where unpaired electrons are not present are diamagnetic in nature.

FeCN63- ,   Fe+3  3d5 → t2g2,2  eg1, paramagnetic (one unpaired electron)

FeCN64-,  Fe+2 3d6→t2g6eg0 ,diamagnetic 

TiCN63-,  Ti+3(3d1)→t2g1 , Paramagnetic (1 unpaired electron)

NiCN42-, Ni+2(3d8)→ Diamagnetic

CoCN63-,   Co+3(3d6)→t2g6, diamagnetic

solution:
In facial isomer, three Cl atoms present on one face of the octahedron. Similarly, three ammonia molecules are present on one face of the octahedron. All 3 same ligands have 90° bond angle between them.

The Cl-Co-Cl bond angle in above octahedral complex is 90°

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
List-I
Electronic configuration of tetrahedral metal ion		List-II
Crystal Field Stabilization Energy ($\Delta_t$)
A. $d^2$I. $-0.6$
B. $d^4$II. $-0.8$
C. $d^6$III. $-1.2$
D. $d^8$IV. $-0.4$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
List-I
Electronic configuration of tetrahedral metal ion		List-II
Crystal Field Stabilization Energy ($\Delta_t$)
A. $d^2$I. $-0.6$
B. $d^4$II. $-0.8$
C. $d^6$III. $-1.2$
D. $d^8$IV. $-0.4$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
In a tetrahedral crystal field, the d-orbitals split into a lower energy $e$ set and a higher energy $t_2$ set. The energy of an electron in the $e$ orbital is $-0.6 \Delta_t$ and in the $t_2$ orbital is $+0.4 \Delta_t$. Since $\Delta_t$ is generally small, tetrahedral complexes form high-spin configurations.

The Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) is given by:
$\text{CFSE} = (-0.6 n_e + 0.4 n_{t_2}) \Delta_t$

For $d^2$: The configuration is $e^2 t_2^0$.
$\text{CFSE} = 2 \times (-0.6) + 0 = -1.2 \Delta_t$ (A $\rightarrow$ III)

For $d^4$: The configuration is $e^2 t_2^2$.
$\text{CFSE} = 2 \times (-0.6) + 2 \times (0.4) = -1.2 + 0.8 = -0.4 \Delta_t$ (B $\rightarrow$ IV)

For $d^6$: The configuration is $e^3 t_2^3$.
$\text{CFSE} = 3 \times (-0.6) + 3 \times (0.4) = -1.8 + 1.2 = -0.6 \Delta_t$ (C $\rightarrow$ I)

For $d^8$: The configuration is $e^4 t_2^4$.
$\text{CFSE} = 4 \times (-0.6) + 4 \times (0.4) = -2.4 + 1.6 = -0.8 \Delta_t$ (D $\rightarrow$ II)

Therefore, the correct match is A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II

q: Identity the incorrect pair from the following:
question: Identity the incorrect pair from the following:
solution:
Wacker process:

The process of oxidation of terminal alkenes to aldehydes and ketones by using Palladium and Copper compounds as catalysts is called the Wacker process.

Palladium (II) chloride and Copper (II) chloride are used in this process along with Oxygen and water.

CH2=CH2 →O2,H2OPdCl2,CuCl2CH3CHO

The catalyst used in Wacker's process is PdCl2

q: Consider that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mn>6</mn></msup></math> metal ion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></mfenced></math> forms a complex with aqua ligands, and the spin only magnetic moment of the complex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.90</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>BM</mi></math>. The geometry and the crystal field stabilization energy of the complex is :
question: Consider that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mn>6</mn></msup></math> metal ion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></mfenced></math> forms a complex with aqua ligands, and the spin only magnetic moment of the complex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.90</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>BM</mi></math>. The geometry and the crystal field stabilization energy of the complex is :
solution:
Since spin only magnetic moment is 4.90 BM so number of unpaired electrons must be 4. There are two possibilities:

(1) If the complex is octahedral, then it has to be high spin complex with configuration t2g2,1,1eg1,1, in that case CFSE=4×-0.4Δ0+2×0.6Δ0=-0.4Δ0
(2) If the complex is tetrahedral then its electronic configuration will be =egt2g2,11,1,1 and CFSE will be=3X -0.6Δt+3X0.4Δt=-0.6Δt

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct order of the complexes $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)\right]^{3+}$ (A),
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ (B),
$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}(\mathrm{C})$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoCl}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{D})$ in terms wavelength of light absorbed is :
question:
The correct order of the complexes $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)\right]^{3+}$ (A),
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ (B),
$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}(\mathrm{C})$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoCl}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{D})$ in terms wavelength of light absorbed is :
solution:
We know $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{h} v=\frac{\mathrm{hC}}{\lambda}$
$\mathrm{E} \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$
Here all Co in +3 oxidation state.
So, as the ligand field strength $\uparrow, \operatorname{CFSE} \uparrow$
Order of field strength of ligand :
$\mathrm{CN} \gt \mathrm{NH}_3 \gt \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \gt \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
CFSE order : $\mathrm{C} \gt \mathrm{B} \gt \mathrm{A} \gt \mathrm{D}$
Wavelength order: $\mathrm{D} \gt \mathrm{A} \gt \mathrm{B} \gt \mathrm{C}$

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>CoF</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> are respectively known as:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>CoF</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> are respectively known as:
options: Spin free Complex, Spin paired Complex
Spin paired Complex, Spin free Complex
Outer orbital Complex, Inner orbital Complex
Inner orbital Complex, Spin paired Complex
solution:
In the complex CoNH363+ Ammonia is strong field ligand and electronic configuration of 

Co3+⇒3 d6t2 g6,eg0

Hybridisation: d2sp3

Pairing of electrons takes place due to the strong field ligand. Hence, it is inner orbital complex (spin paired complex).

In the complex CoF63- fluoride is weak field ligand and electronic configuration of  Co3+ ⇒3d6t2g4,eg2.

Hybridisation: sp3d2

Pairing of electrons not takes place due to the weak field ligand. Hence, it is outer orbital complex (spin free complex).

Hence option B is the answer.

q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Dimethyl glyoxime forms a six membered covalent chelate when treated with NiCl2 solution in presence of NH4OH.
Statement (II) : Prussian blue precipitate contains iron both in +2 and +3 oxidation states.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Dimethyl glyoxime forms a six membered covalent chelate when treated with NiCl2 solution in presence of NH4OH.
Statement (II) : Prussian blue precipitate contains iron both in +2 and +3 oxidation states.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
According to Statement I,

On addition of  dimethylglyoxime (DMG) to nickel ion solution and on adding  little bit of ammonia to make solution basic, it will give a red precipitate. This is a very specific test for nickel +2 cation.

The reaction is as follows,

Ni2++NH4OH+dmg→

2 hydrogen bonded chelatesare  formed.

According to Statement II,

IIIFe4 IIFeCN63

Prussian Blue

IN this complex Fe is  at +3 as well as +2 oxidation state. Where ionisation sphere is Fe3+ and coordination sphere has Fe2+

Hence option A is the answer..

The increasing order of ligand strength of the four ligands is
question: The octahedral complex of a metal ion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> with four monodentate ligands <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math><sub> </sub>absorb wavelengths in the region of red, green, yellow and blue, respectively. The increasing order of ligand strength of the four ligands is
solution:
The colors of wavelengths are absorbed in decreasing order of wavelengths.

λ red > λ yellow > λ green > λ blue

as ΔE=hcλ

Δ E Red < Δ E Yellow < Δ E Green < Δ E Blue

L1 < L3 < L2 < L4

As the order of ligand field strength provides the order of relative crystal field splitting energies.

[At. no. of Co=27]
question:
The spin-only magnetic moment value for the complex Co(CN)64- is...... BM. (nearest integer value)
[At. no. of Co=27]
solution:
Co(CN)64-
x+6×(-1)=-4
x=+2
Co2+:[Ar]3 d7 and CN- is a strong field ligand which can pair electron of central atom.

It has one unpaired electron (n) in 4 d-subshell. So spin only magnetic moment (μ)=
n(n+2)B·M
where n= number of unpaired electrons.
μ=3 B.M
μ=1.73 BM ≃2 BM

subject: Chemistry
q: The theory that can completely/properly explain the nature of bonding in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Ni</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
question: The theory that can completely/properly explain the nature of bonding in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Ni</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
There is synergic bonding present in NiCO4.

As CO is neutral ligand, so oxidation state of nickel is zero i.e.Ni0.

So, electronic configuration of Ni0 =Ar3d8 4s2

As CO is strong field ligand, it will back paired the 4s electrons to 3d orbitals.

So in complex electronic configuration of Ni0 =Ar 3d10 4s0 here inner d orbital is filled.

In ligand CO (carbonyl) the anti-bonding orbital is vacant.

CO: σ1s2, σ*1s2, σ2s2, σ*2s2, σ2pz2, πpx2=πpy2, π*px0=π*py0

The fully filled inner d-orbital of central metal atom gives it's electrons to vacant π anti-bonding orbital, this is called back π bonding, due to this back bonding bond between metal and ligand is very strong.

This back π bonding is also called synergic bonding and this synergic bonding is explained by molecular orbital theory.

Carbon monoxide CO  made synergic bonding with Haemoglobin in are blood, as synergic bonding is very strong, so oxygen taking capacity of haemoglobin decrease and it cause death.

question:

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider the transition metal ions $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ and all form low spin octahedral complexes. The correct decreasing order of unpaired electrons in their respective d-orbitals of the complexes is
question: Consider the transition metal ions $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ and all form low spin octahedral complexes. The correct decreasing order of unpaired electrons in their respective d-orbitals of the complexes is
solution:
For low spin octahedral complexes, electrons fill the $t_{2g}$ orbitals first before pairing or moving to $e_g$ orbitals. The configuration depends on the number of d-electrons.
For $Cr^{3+}$: Electronic configuration is $3d^3$. In an octahedral field, the configuration is $t_{2g}^3 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons ($n$) = 3.
For $Mn^{3+}$: Electronic configuration is $3d^4$. For low spin, the configuration is $t_{2g}^4 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons ($n$) = 2.
For $Fe^{3+}$: Electronic configuration is $3d^5$. For low spin, the configuration is $t_{2g}^5 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons ($n$) = 1.
For $Co^{3+}$: Electronic configuration is $3d^6$. For low spin, the configuration is $t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons ($n$) = 0.
Comparing the number of unpaired electrons:
$Cr^{3+} (3) > Mn^{3+} (2) > Fe^{3+} (1) > Co^{3+} (0)$.
The correct decreasing order is $Cr^{3+} > Mn^{3+} > Fe^{3+} > Co^{3+}$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The statements that are incorrect about the nickel(II) complex of dimethylglyoxime are :
A. It is red in colour.
B. It has a high solubility in water at $\mathrm{pH}=9$.
C. The Ni ion has two unpaired d-electrons.
D. The $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{Ni}-\mathrm{N}$ bond angle is almost close to $90^{\circ}$.
E. The complex contains four five-membered metallacycles (metal containing rings).
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The statements that are incorrect about the nickel(II) complex of dimethylglyoxime are :
A. It is red in colour.
B. It has a high solubility in water at $\mathrm{pH}=9$.
C. The Ni ion has two unpaired d-electrons.
D. The $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{Ni}-\mathrm{N}$ bond angle is almost close to $90^{\circ}$.
E. The complex contains four five-membered metallacycles (metal containing rings).
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Statement A is incorrect as the complex is yellow-green, not red.
Statement B is incorrect because the complex shows low solubility in water, especially at neutral pH.
Statement C is incorrect because Ni(II) in square planar geometry has all d-electrons paired (d^8 configuration), not unpaired electrons.
Statement D is correct regarding the N-Ni-N bond angle being approximately 90° in square planar geometry.
Statement E is correct as the complex contains two five-membered metallacycles (chelate rings) per dimethylglyoxime molecule.
The incorrect statements are B, C, and E.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as “Assertion A” and the other is labelled as “Reason R”

Assertion A : In the complex Ni(CO)4 and Fe(CO)5, the metals have zero oxidation state.

Reason R : Low oxidation states are found when a complex has ligands capable of π-donor character in addition to the σ-bonding.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as “Assertion A” and the other is labelled as “Reason R”

Assertion A : In the complex Ni(CO)4 and Fe(CO)5, the metals have zero oxidation state.

Reason R : Low oxidation states are found when a complex has ligands capable of π-donor character in addition to the σ-bonding.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: A is correct but R is not correct
A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
The ligand carbon monoxide has no charge, hence metals have zero oxidation state in both Ni(CO)4 and Fe(CO)5. Low oxidation states are found when a complex has ligands capable of π-acceptor character in addition to the σ-bonding.

In the complexes, NiCO4 & FeCO5, the ligand CO is π-acceptor character in addition to the σ-bonding.
